summary: g-api-mcp is a Python 3.11+ stdio MCP server using FastMCP (mcp[cli] >=1.26.0); all tools are async functions decorated with @mcp.tool() registered at import time
The project is purely request-response — no polling, scheduling, background threads, or file watchers exist anywhere in the codebase
Auth uses Windows Credential Locker via keyring (DPAPI); credential loading is thread-safe via GoogleCredentialManager singleton; Tasks API is already fully wired with all CRUD tools

The cleanest extension point for a sync feature is a new tasks_sync_to_vault.py module following the existing tool pattern, callable on-demand by Claude Code; for scheduled operation the /schedule skill or Windows Task Scheduler would drive it

Deduplication state must be persisted externally to the MCP server process (which is stateless between invocations); a small JSON state file tracking last-synced task updated timestamps keyed by task ID is the lightest viable approach

findings.key_finding: Entry point is a FastMCP stdio server. Tools are registered by importing module files as side effects; each module uses @mcp.tool() decorator. Server starts with mcp.run() which blocks in a stdio read loop — no scheduler, no background thread, no periodic work. This is a pure synchronous request-response MCP server.

All 7 Google Tasks operations (list tasklists, list tasks, get task, create, update, complete, delete) are implemented as async @mcp.tool() functions. The tasks_list_tasks tool supports due_min/due_max filtering and cursor pagination, and exposes the 'updated' timestamp on every thin task summary. These fields are exactly what a sync/deduplication mechanism needs to detect changes.

GoogleCredentialManager is a module-level singleton that loads the OAuth2 refresh token from Windows Credential Locker (keyring) and auto-refreshes access tokens. It is thread-safe and designed to be shared across tools. A sync script in the same package would import cred_manager and get authenticated Google API access for free with no additional auth plumbing.

Project is installed as an editable package (pip install -e .) with a g-api-mcp console script entry point. Adding a new console script (e.g., g-api-mcp-sync) is one line in [project.scripts]. The package layout in src/g_api_mcp/ makes it trivial to add sync.py alongside gmail.py, calendar.py, tasks.py.

Claude Code registration runs the server as a subprocess via python -m g_api_mcp.server with PYTHONPATH set. A standalone sync script could be run separately from the MCP server — it shares the same package and credentials without conflicting with the running server process.

The envelope pattern (build_envelope / error_envelope) is a thin utility with no server-side state. If a sync MCP tool is added, it should return the same envelope schema for consistency. If the sync runs as a standalone script (not an MCP tool), the envelope is not needed.

No scheduled tasks, polling loops, file watchers, cron utilities, APScheduler, asyncio.sleep loops, or background task infrastructure exist anywhere in the codebase. The project has zero precedent for autonomous periodic execution — this is a gap that must be filled by the trigger mechanism chosen for the sync feature.

The 'updated' field (RFC 3339 timestamp) is surfaced in thin task summaries from tasks_list_tasks. This is the key field for change detection: a sync script can compare stored last-seen 'updated' values against the API response to identify new or modified tasks without fetching full task details first.

architecture_decisions.recommendation: New module src/g_api_mcp/sync.py + console script entry point g-api-mcp-sync. Reuses auth, cred_manager, and Google API patterns. Expose as both a standalone runnable script AND an optional @mcp.tool() for on-demand Claude-triggered syncs. Do NOT put it in a separate repo — the shared auth singleton is too valuable.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Minimizes new infrastructure. Auth is solved. Pattern is established. A console script can be driven by any scheduler independently of the MCP server process.

options: Manual MCP tool call from Claude
Standalone daemon with asyncio polling loop
architecture_decisions.recommendation: Windows Task Scheduler as primary trigger for reliable background execution; MCP tool call as secondary for on-demand/manual syncs from Claude Code. Skip the /schedule skill (requires a running Claude Code session) and daemon (adds complexity, no crash recovery).
architecture_decisions.rationale: Windows Task Scheduler is always-on, survives reboots, has built-in logging, and can run headlessly. A 15-30 minute interval is appropriate for a 'Gemini added tasks on phone' use case. The MCP tool path enables manual triggered syncs from Claude without needing to touch the scheduler.

options: Vault note frontmatter as ground truth
architecture_decisions.recommendation: JSON state file at a well-known path (e.g., %APPDATA%/g-api-mcp/sync-state.json or project root sync-state.json). Track {task_id: {updated: RFC3339, vault_path: str}}. This is sufficient for the scale of a personal task list.
architecture_decisions.rationale: SQLite is overkill for a personal task list. Always-overwrite breaks vault edits. Vault frontmatter as sole ground truth requires a vault read on every sync (expensive). JSON state file is readable, diffable, git-ignorable, and covers all required operations: skip-if-unchanged, detect-updates, find-deletions.

options: Two-way: vault changes synced back to Google Tasks
architecture_decisions.recommendation: One-way (Google Tasks → vault) for v1. Two-way sync requires conflict resolution and is significantly more complex.
architecture_decisions.rationale: The stated use case is 'Gemini populates tasks on phone, they appear in vault.' No vault-to-Google flow is needed initially. Vault notes can be edited freely; sync will not overwrite vault-side changes if update detection uses the task's Google-side 'updated' timestamp.

architecture_decisions.recommendation: %APPDATA%/g-api-mcp/sync-state.json — consistent with where auth token would go on non-Windows-Credential-Locker systems; keeps project root clean; survives project re-clones.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Project root is gitignored for state files but risky if user ever cleans the directory. APPDATA is the right place for user-specific runtime state on Windows.

gaps: No existing polling/scheduling infrastructure in g-api-mcp — trigger mechanism is a net-new component
Google Tasks API does not have push notifications (webhooks) — polling is the only option; optimal polling interval needs to be determined based on acceptable latency
tasks_list_tasks returns 'updated' in thin summaries but the exact RFC 3339 format precision needs validation to ensure reliable string comparison for change detection

Vault write conventions (note schema, frontmatter fields, folder location) are not documented in this codebase — Track 2 (Vault task conventions) must be consulted before implementing the vault write side
No error recovery / retry logic pattern exists in the current codebase beyond returning error envelopes — sync script will need its own retry strategy for transient Google API failures

The tasks_list_tasks tool does not expose an 'updatedMin' filter (Google Tasks API supports this) — adding this parameter would make incremental sync much more efficient by only fetching tasks updated since last sync
Windows Task Scheduler setup is manual — no automation or self-registration script exists
delta_queries: Track 2: What is the vault note schema for tasks? (frontmatter fields, folder, naming convention)
Track 1: Does Google Tasks API support updatedMin parameter in tasks.list? Verify exact parameter name and behavior
Track 3: What transformation is needed from Google Task fields to vault frontmatter/body?
Verify: does tasks_list_tasks currently pass updatedMin to the API, or would that require a new parameter in tasks.py?
Check: what is the exact format of the 'updated' field returned by Google Tasks API to confirm reliable timestamp comparison
Investigate: can a standalone Python script import g_api_mcp.auth.cred_manager without starting the MCP server? (Should work given module-level singleton pattern)
